coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission CdTe/AgInS2 photoluminescence probe coupled to neural network data processing for the simultaneous determination of folic acid and iron (II)
coredata.dc:creator: Castro, Rafael C.
Ribeiro, David S.M.
Páscoa, Ricardo N.M.J.
Soares, José X.
Mazivila, Sarmento J.
Santos, João L.M.
coredata.dc:description: This work focused on the combination of CdTe and AgInS2 quantum dots in a dual-emission nanoprobe for the simultaneous determination of folic acid and Fe(II) in pharmaceutical formulations. The surface chemistry of the used QDs was amended with suitable capping ligands to obtain appropriate reactivity in terms of selectivity and sensitivity towards the target analytes. The implementation of PL-based sensing schemes combining multiple QDs of different nature, excited at the same wavelength and emitting at different ones, allowed to obtain a specific analyte-response profile. The first-order fluorescence data obtained from the whole emission spectra of the CdTe/AgInS2 combined nanoprobe upon interaction with folic acid and Fe(II) were processed by using chemometric tools, namely partial least-squares (PLS) and artificial neural network (ANN). This enabled to circumvent the selectivity issues commonly associated with the use of QDs prone to indiscriminate interaction with multiple species, which impair reliable and accurate quantification in complex matrices samples. ANN demonstrated to be the most efficient chemometric model for the simultaneous determination of both analytes in binary mixtures and pharmaceutical formulations due to the non-linear relationship between analyte concentration and fluorescence data that it could handle. The R2 P and SEP% obtained for both analytes quantification in pharmaceutical formulations through ANN modelling ranged from 0.92 to 0.99 and 5.7–9.1%, respectively. The obtained results revealed that the developed approach is able to quantify, with high reliability and accuracy, more than one analyte in complex mixtures and real samples with pharmaceutical interest.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission CdTe/AgInS2 photoluminescence probe coupled to neural network data processing for the simultaneous determination of folic acid and iron (II)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: LAQV, REQUIMTE, Department of Chemical Sciences, Laboratory of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Porto, Rua de Jorge Viterbo Ferreira Nº 228, 4050-313, Porto, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Rua de Jorge Viterbo Ferreira Nº 228

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: LAQV, REQUIMTE, Department of Chemical Sciences, Laboratory of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Porto, Rua de Jorge Viterbo Ferreira Nº 228, 4050-313, Porto, Portugal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work focused on the combination of CdTe and AgInS2 quantum dots in a dual-emission nanoprobe for the simultaneous determination of folic acid and Fe(II) in pharmaceutical formulations. The surface chemistry of the used QDs was amended with suitable capping ligands to obtain appropriate reactivity in terms of selectivity and sensitivity towards the target analytes. The implementation of PL-based sensing schemes combining multiple QDs of different nature, excited at the same wavelength and emitting at different ones, allowed to obtain a specific analyte-response profile. The first-order fluorescence data obtained from the whole emission spectra of the CdTe/AgInS2 combined nanoprobe upon interaction with folic acid and Fe(II) were processed by using chemometric tools, namely partial least-squares (PLS) and artificial neural network (ANN). This enabled to circumvent the selectivity issues commonly associated with the use of QDs prone to indiscriminate interaction with multiple species, which impair reliable and accurate quantification in complex matrices samples.

ANN demonstrated to be the most efficient chemometric model for the simultaneous determination of both analytes in binary mixtures and pharmaceutical formulations due to the non-linear relationship between analyte concentration and fluorescence data that it could handle. The R2 P and SEP% obtained for both analytes quantification in pharmaceutical formulations through ANN modelling ranged from 0.92 to 0.99 and 5.7–9.1%, respectively. The obtained results revealed that the developed approach is able to quantify, with high reliability and accuracy, more than one analyte in complex mixtures and real samples with pharmaceutical interest.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid (FA) is a natural water-soluble B vitamin (vitamin B9) which is involved in numerous biological processes, such as transport and enzymatic processing of carbon units for amino acid and nucleic acid metabolism, cell growth, reproduction, protein metabolism and in promoting erythrocyte formation and maturation [1,2]. This vitamin is widely present in food matrices such as green leafy vegetables, fruits, grains, legumes and dairy products [3]. FA deficiency is associated with many diseases namely, fetal neural tube defects, cardiovascular disease, megaloblastic anemia and cancers [2].

Since the human body is unable to synthesize FA and when the daily intake of vitamin B9 do not provide the essential amount for maintaining adequate health conditions (7–42 nmol L−1), it may be necessary to resort to dietary supplements and/or pharmaceutical formulations to achieve this goal [4,5]. Moreover, this vitamin is usually prescribed for pregnant women to prevent fetal neural tube defects. In many circumstances, FA is combined with iron for the prevention and treatment of some anemias which are a worldwide public-health problem, affecting about a quarter of the world’s population [6]. Iron is essential for a multiplicity of biological mechanisms that involve the erythropoietic function, oxidative metabolism, cellular immune responses, among others [7]. The combined use of FA and iron allows to increase the haemoglobin and haematocrit levels while reducing the need for blood transfusions [8]. It is worth mentioning that high levels of iron, usually associated with a substantial ingestion of this element, can cause the saturation of transferrin and, as consequence, the excess of free iron remaining in the blood circulation can be toxic to several target organs [9,10].

From the aforementioned reasons it can be perceived why the development of simple and fast methodologies for the determination of FA and iron has attracted a great research awareness. Among the available works focusing the determination of iron and folic acid, the use of semiconductors quantum dots (QDs) as fluorescent probes can be highlighted. The quenching effect of different kind of QDs has been explored for the individual determination of FA [4,11,12] and iron [13–15]. However, the development of a fluorometric nanoprobe for the simultaneous determination of FA and iron has not been exploited hitherto.

The use of QDs of different composition, in ternary and binary arrangements, and stabilized with distinct thiol-based organic compounds have been widely exploited as photoluminescence (PL) probes and/or sensors in a panoply of analytical applications [16–22]. Indeed, both ternary and binary quantum dots have in common interesting optical properties that make them valuable nanomaterials to be applied in chemical analysis. These include: i) strong capacity to absorb electromagnetic radiations with high molar attenuation coefficients in a wide range of wavelengths, allowing the excitation of multiple QDs of different emissions at a single wavelength; ii) high quantum yields (QY) that provide improved sensitivity; iii) highly tunable surface chemistry that enable the adjustment of their reactivity towards a target analyte [23–25].

In most of the reported works resorting to QDs as PL probes for analytical purposes, selectivity is a critical parameter that raises noteworthy concerns among the scientific community involved in the development of QD-based chemo-sensing strategies for detection of one or more analytes in the same analysis, or for the monitoring of complex sample matrices. In fact, in many cases, without specific recognition mechanisms, QDs are normally susceptible to establish non-selective interactions with multiple species due to their high reactivity which impairs the accuracy of the obtained results. This drawback has been overcome by employing distinct expeditious approaches with the purpose of circumventing the underlying lack of selectivity, which usually comprise: i) QDs surface functionalization with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) tailored for sensing a specific target analyte [26–31]; ii) bioconjugation with aptamers and/or antibodies [32–38]; iii) cooperative combination of QDs with other nanoparticles to implement distinct donor–acceptor pairs in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based assays [19,39–41]; and (iv) the simultaneous utilization of multiple QDs with different size and/or composition as a combined nanoprobe in multiplexed assays [25,29,37,42] and ratiometric sensing schemes [43–46].

Regarding the latter approach, the combination of multiple QDs of different composition and size in a dual emission probe system can effectively provide a specific analyte-response profile. The traditional QDs-based PL methodologies rely on one-direction signal change (either by QDs PL quenching or enhancing) which can be easily interfered by several factors. On contrary, the use of two different QDs with distinct affinities to a given analyte in a combined probe can enable a multi-point detection which afford additional and more useful information regarding analyte determination, circumventing the possible occurrence of signal artifacts.

The use of combined nanoprobes resorting to chemometric models for extracting useful analytical information and dealing with more than one analyte in the same sample, with more or less complexity, has been successfully employed [25,42,47,48]. Additionally, this synergy between the use of combined nanoprobes with chemometric models for discriminant analysis, based on partial least-squares – discriminant analysis (PLS–DA) has been also explored as a high sensitive and specific methodology for the recognition of different samples with food interest [49,50] and for the identification of possible adulterants in a real sample [51].

Herein, the interaction of FA and Fe(II) with both CdTe QDs capped with cysteamine (CA), cysteine (CYS), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA) and glutathione (GSH) and AgInS2 QDs stabilized with MPA, was thoroughly assessed and their reactivity was compared for each assayed analyte. The chemometric models based on linear partial least-squares (PLS) and artificial neural network (ANN) were employed for the processing of first-order fluorescence data obtained from the whole emission spectra of the CdTe/AgInS2 combined nanoprobe upon interaction with FA and Fe(II). The proposed approach allows the simultaneous determination of both analytes in a binary mixture and in real samples with pharmaceutical interest.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Reagents and solutions
ce:section.ce:para: All solutions and standards were prepared by resorting to chemicals of analytical grade without any treatment or further purification and by using ultrapure water from a Milli-Q system (conductivity ≤0.1 μS cm−1).

A 20.0 μmol L−1 of folic acid intermediate solution was obtained through the dissolution of 8.83 mg of the vitamin B9 standard reagent (C19H19N7O6, Sigma-Aldrich®, ≥97%, St. Louis, MO, USA) in 1000 mL of ultrapure water with the aid of an ultrasonic bath system. The preparation of a 500.0 μmol L−1 Fe2+ intermediate solution involved the weighing and dissolving of 69.50 mg of iron(II) sulphate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O, Sigma-Aldrich®, ≥98%, St. Louis, MO, USA) in 500 mL of deionized water. The working standard solutions of vitamin B9 and Fe(II) were prepared by suitable dilutions from their intermediate solutions.

The different thiol-capped CdTe QDs solutions used in the analysis, were prepared by appropriate weighing and dissolution of the respective powder of the as-prepared QDs which were previously precipitated from the crude solution obtained in the aqueous synthesis. The AgInS2 QDs solution involved in the analytical assays was prepared through the proper dilution of the crude solution obtained from the synthesis without any additional purification process.

The sample solutions of two different commercially available pharmaceutical formulations were prepared by weighing and powdering a representative number of tablets. One of the commercially available formulation contains only FA (sample 1) and the other contains FA (1 mg) and ferrous sulphate (288 mg) at very different amounts (sample 2). For sample 1, an amount containing 10 mg FA was accurately weighed and dissolved in ultrapure water using an ultrasonic bath for 60 min. For sample 2, an amount containing 576 mg of FeSO4 and 2 mg of FA was weighed and dissolved by using mechanical stirring for 2 h. Thereafter, the solutions were filtered through a vacuum filter system into 1000 mL volumetric flasks and the final volume was completed with ultrapure water. Both samples, 1 and 2, were spiked with Fe and FA, respectively, to allow the simultaneous determination of both analytes in the same analysis. At the end, five different dilutions of each sample solution were performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aqueous synthesis of CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: All reagents involved in the aqueous synthesis of CdTe QDs were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich® (St. Louis, MO, USA) and were used as received.

The synthesis of CdTe QDs capped with 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), l-cysteine (CYS) and mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA) were performed according to the aqueous phase route proposed by Zou et al. [52] with some modifications. This procedure involved two steps. The first step consisted on the reduction of tellurium (Te powder, 200 mesh, 99.8%) with sodium borohydride (NaBH4, ≥96%) in nitrogen saturated water to produce sodium hydrogen telluride (NaHTe). The resulting solution, after the complete consumption of tellurium, was transferred into a three neck flask containing 4.0 mmol of cadmium chloride (CdCl2, 99.99%) and 6.8 mmol of MPA (C3H6O2S, ≥99%) in 100 mL nitrogen saturated solution. The molar ratio of Cd:Te:MPA was fixed at 1:0.1:1.7. The solution pH was adjusted to 11.5 by dropwise addition of 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH solution. The required size of CdTe QDs was adjusted by controlling the refluxing time. The CdTe QDs capped with CYS and MSA were prepared by using the same procedure, but with distinct molar ratios and pH. Indeed, the molar ratios of Cd:Te:capping ligand were 1:0.05:2.4 for CYS (C3H7NO2S, 97%) and 1:0.33:3 for MSA (C4H6O4S, 97%), at pH 10.5 and 11.0, respectively.

For the synthesis of l-glutathione reduced (GSH)-capped CdTe QDs, a one-step synthetic route proposed by Qian [53] was employed with few modifications. Briefly, into a single reaction pot, cadmium chloride, sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3, 99%) and GSH (C10H17N3O6S, ≥98%) were mixed in a molar ratio of 1:0.2:1.2 (Cd:Te:GSH) with sodium borohydride and sodium citrate tribasic dihydrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O, ≥99%) in 100 mL of ultrapure water. The pH of the resulting solution was adjusted to 10.5 with 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH and heated with vigorous stirring at 100 °C under reflux for nanoparticle growth.

All the aforesaid synthesized QDs crude solutions were precipitated with absolute ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.8%, Panreac, Barcelona, Spain) in order to remove the excess of reactants. Then, the precipitates were centrifuged, dried in vacuum, kept in amber flasks and protected from light, for posterior use.

The synthesis of CdTe QDs stabilized with cysteamine (CA) was carried out using a one-step synthetic route assisted by microwave irradiation proposed by Ribeiro et al. [54] with some important modifications. In this procedure, hydroxylamine hydrochloride (NH2OH·HCl, 98.0%) was used instead of citrate to avoid the precipitation of the nanoparticles. Thus, 1.25 mmol of CdCl2 and 2.50 mmol of CA (C2H7NS, ≥98.0%) were mixed in 125 mL of ultrapure water. Then, the mixture was transferred to a second beaker containing separately, 0.38 mmol of Na2TeO3, 6.0 mmol of NaBH4, and 1.25 mmol of NH2OH. The molar ratio of Cd:Te:CA was fixed at 1.0:0.3:2.0 and the solution pH was adjusted to 5 with a 1.0 mol L−1 HCl. The required CA-capped CdTe QDs was obtained by fixing the MW irradiation time at 10 min and the temperature at 80 °C. In the preliminary assays, this QDs was used as prepared without any purification treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the aqueous synthesis of AgInS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of MPA-capped AgInS2 QDs, all reagents were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich® (St. Louis, MO, USA) or Acros organics™ (New Jersey, USA).

In a 35-mL reaction vessel, 62.0 μmol of silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.9999%), 0.9 mmol of ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 28.0–30.0% NH3 basis) and 1.0 mmol of 3-mercaptopropionic acid (C3H6O2S, 99%) were dissolved with 25 mL of ultrapure water under vigorous magnetic stirring. Then, 0.2 mmol of indium chloride (InCl3, 99.995%) were added dropwise and the pH was adjusted to 8.5 by dropwise adding of NH4OH solution. After that, 0.2 mmol of sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O, 98+%) was rapidly added and the resulting solution was heated, under microwave irradiation, at 100 °C during 50 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric procedure
ce:section.ce:para: For the preliminary and quantitative assays, all synthetized QDs were diluted in order to obtain a similar fluorescence intensity in all the obtained spectra. Thus, for the CdTe QDs stabilized with CA, CYS, MSA, MPA and GSH the final concentrations obtained were 0.185, 0.392, 0.309, 0.156 and 0.229 μmol L−1, respectively. In the case of MPA-capped AIS QDs, a dilution in a ratio of 1:100 (v/v) of the crude solution were used aiming the obtaining of a similar fluorescence intensity regarding the binary QDs.

Typically, the procedure used for the assays involved, in a first stage, the addition into Eppendorf tubes of the required amounts of FA, Fe(II) and deionized water up to a volume of 1800 μL. Subsequently, 100 μL of CdTe QDs and 100 μL of AIS QDs intermediate solutions were sequentially added completing a final volume of 2 mL. Immediately after the addition of the nanoprobe the resulting mixture was stirred and transferred into a quartz cell (1 cm of optical path). The emission spectra, at fixed λ ex = 400 nm and by using slit widths of excitation and emission of 5.0 nm, were recorded in a λ em range from 480 to 800 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: The ternary AIS QDs and CA-capped CdTe QDs were synthesized in a CEM Discover SP® Microwave system containing an automated pressure control/sensing system (ActiVent™), an active cooling system (PowerMAX™) and an integrated infrared (IR) sensor. All the operations of this microwave synthesizer were performed through a computer using Synergy™ software (Matthews, NC, US).
A pH-meter GLP 22 (CRISON) was used for the pH measurements and a Thermo Electron Jouan BR4I refrigerated centrifuge (Waltham MA, USA) was employed for the separation of precipitated QDs.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Fluorolog®-3 spectrofluorimeter (Horiba Jobin Yvon, NJ, USA) while the absorbance spectra were obtained using a double-beam Jasco V-660 spectrophotometer (Jasco, Easton, MD, USA). The measurement of the quantum yield (QY) of all QDs used in this work was performed in a Quantaurus QY—C11347-11 Absolute QY Spectrometer equipped with an integration sphere (Hamamatsu, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Data analysis
ce:section.ce:para: A principal component analysis (PCA) was performed for the detection of outliers and common patterns [55]. PLS regression [56] and ANN modelling [57] were tested for the development of quantification models regarding iron and folic acid concentration. The data was previously mean centered before PCA and PLS modelling. Both PCA and PLS were executed in Matlab R2014a version 8.3 (MathWorks, Natick, USA) using PLS Toolbox version 8.2.1 (Eigenvector Research Inc., Wenatchee, USA).

Regarding PLS, the PLS-1 algorithm was selected for the development of quantification models. For the optimization of PLS models, the initial data set (not containing the commercial samples) was randomly divided in 70% for calibration (23 samples) and 30% for validation (10 samples). Several pre-processing techniques, including standard normal variate (SNV), Savitzky-Golay (SG) with different filter widths, polynomial orders and derivatives and log10 transformation were tested individually and in combination aiming to optimize the developed models. The optimal number of latent variables (LV) was estimated through leave-one-sample-out cross validation method to prevent model over-fitting. The root mean square error of calibration (RMSEC), cross-validation (RMSECV) and prediction (RMSEP) were estimated according to the following equation:

The Pearson’s correlation coefficient was also calculated to compare experimental values with the estimations. The developed PLS models were evaluated during the optimization using RMSEC, RMSECV and coefficient of determination of cross-validation (R2 CV). After the optimization, the validation samples were projected to test the accuracy of the respective PLS model. At this point, the PLS models were also evaluated through RMSEP, coefficient of determination of prediction (R2 P) and range error ratio (RER). The RER was calculated using the following equation:

The ANN modelling using a multi-layer feed-forward network with error back-propagation learning rule was performed through MVC1_GUI, a MATLAB graphical user interface for first-order multivariate calibration [58], available for download in Ref. [59], in Matlab R2014a version 8.3. The ANN used consists in three layers of neurons (also known as architecture), namely: the input layer, the hidden layer and the one output layer. This indicates that for each parameter modelled an independent ANN was built. For the optimization of ANN models, the initial data set (not containing the commercial samples) was randomly divided in 70% for calibration (23 samples) and 30% for validation (10 samples). The optimization of ANN models involved testing different input and hidden layer neurons number (by trial and error for both parameters) and several pre-processing techniques (namely, multiplicative scatter correction (MSC), SNV, SG) were also tested individually and in combination. During the optimization, the following parameters, learning rate, momentum and epoch were set to 0.5, 0.5 and 2000, respectively. The application of the pre-processing techniques did not improve the accuracy of the developed ANN models. At the end, the ANNs developed for both iron and acid folic presented the same architecture 4-2-1, comprising four input layer neurons, two hidden layer neurons and one single output neuron. The data in both cases were just mean centered. The ANN models developed during the optimization were evaluated regarding the RMSEC and RMSEC of monitoring (both obtained just with the calibration samples). After the ANN optimization, the validation samples were projected to test the accuracy of the respective ANN model. At this point, the ANN models were also evaluated through the RMSEP, standard error of prediction (SEP%), R2 P, limit-of-determination (LOD) and limit-of-quantification (LOQ). More details about the calculation of these parameters can be found elsewhere [58].

As ANN models yielded better results for the quantification of both parameters, it was decided to test the developed ANN models with two commercial samples. The respective ANN models were evaluated, again, through RMSEP, SEP%, R2 P, LOD and LOQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
QDs optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: Along with size and nature of the nanoparticle, the selection of a capping ligand has an extreme importance on QDs reactivity, as it provides a specific chemical functionality [18,25]. The utilization of QDs capped with organic ligands with distinct terminal functional groups revealed different reactivity towards a given analyte. For that reason, five different CdTe QDs capped with distinct thiol based ligands were synthesized and characterized aiming to obtain information about size and optical properties. The optical properties were assessed through the measurement of UV–VIS absorption and photoluminescence spectra of all QDs involved in the present work which are depicted in Fig. S1 – Supplementary material.

The absorption spectra of all synthesized QDs exhibited wide bands with well resolved absorption maxima for the first excitonic transition (Table 1 ). Using these information, the estimation of nanoparticle sizes and molar concentration of each synthesized QDs was performed by applying the equations proposed by Yu et al. [60]. In Table 1 are also showed the obtained FWHM values which confirmed the narrow and symmetric emission bands of the CdTe QDs showed in Fig. S1 – Supplementary material. This fact demonstrated that QDs were homogeneous and monodisperse. The as-prepared nanoparticles also showed different quantum yields values between 3.2 and 46.5%.

Regarding the AIS ternary QDs, the obtained PL emission band (Fig. S1(f) – Supplementary material) displayed, as expected, a characteristic broad emission band [61] confirmed by the FWHM value, which is ascribed to the distinct relaxation pathways [20]. In fact, in addition to the exciton electron-hole recombination, the occurrence of mid-gap defect states within the band gap, acting either as donor and acceptor states, also contributes to the optical properties of the ternary AIS QDs and substantiates the broader emission band.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary assays
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the PL probe towards the analyte is an essential condition for the development of an analytical methodology. In fact, the low selectivity of QDs was widely reported in scientific literature as the major drawback in the implementation of PL-based sensing methodologies using these nanomaterials.

Recently, the combination of multiple QDs in the same analysis (capped with distinct ligands and emitting at different wavelengths) was highlighted as one efficient strategy to improve a specific analyte-response profile [25,42,44–46,62–64]. Therefore, the presence in the same probe of binary and ternary QDs passivated with capping ligands containing different terminal functional groups, and thus distinct reactivity, can be exploited to enhance the selectivity and sensitivity of the PL probe towards a given analyte.

In this work, in order to optimize a CdTe and AIS combined probe to determinate FA and Fe(II) in pharmaceutical products, the reactivity of five different CdTe QDs with distinct capping ligands such as, cysteamine (CA-CdTe), cysteine (CYS–CdTe), mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA-CdTe), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA-CdTe) and glutathione (GSH-CdTe), and with PL emission maximum in the range of 544–620 was thoroughly evaluated. The interaction of each analyte with AgInS2 QD capped with 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA-AIS) with PL emission at approximately 703 nm was also assessed.

Furthermore, the concentrations of all synthesized QDs were adjusted in order to guarantee similar fluorescence intensity, as referred in subsection 2.4. A similar emission intensity for AIS QDs was obtained by using a 1:100 dilution ratio of the crude solution.
For each individual PL probe, the concentration ranges tested for FA and Fe(II) were 0.05–15 μmol L−1 and 0.03–263 μmol L−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of FA and Fe(II) with MPA capped AgInS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained results (Fig. S2 – Supplementary material) showed that both FA and Fe(II) solutions caused the quenching of the PL emission of AIS QDs and that this effect was concentration dependent, increasing for higher analyte concentrations.

Alongside the PL quenching effect, the interaction between AIS and Fe(II) showed also a red shift of the maximum emission wavelength of up to 29 nm by increasing the cation concentration. The linear relationship between the AIS PL quenching (F0/F) and the concentration of Fe(II) within the range 0.6–66 μmol L−1 was described by the Stern-Volmer equation, F0/F = 0.054 (±0.004) × [Fe2+] + 1.34 (±0.01).

Regarding the interaction with FA, a red shift of the maximum emission wavelength of up to 10 nm was observed when increasing the concentration of the drug. In this case, the interaction with AIS was described by the linear Stern-Vomer equation, F0/F = 0.09 (±0.01) × [FA] + 0.94 (±0.07), for a concentration range of 1–10 μmol L−1.

This study showed the distinct reactivity of AIS for FA and Fe(II), confirmed by the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (Ksv) obtained for the drug which was approximately twice higher than the one obtained for the metal, and demonstrated that FA is a more efficient quencher of MPA-AIS QDs PL emission than Fe(II). However, the metal caused a bathochromic effect with a higher magnitude than one obtained with vitamin B9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of FA and Fe(II) with CA-CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of FA with CA-CdTe QDs caused a progressive inhibition of the PL probe properties for FA concentrations of up to 2.0 μmol L−1. This effect, which was already verified by Ramírez-Herrera et al. [11], was accompanied by a blue shift of the maximum emission wavelength of about 11 nm (Fig. S3 – Supplementary material). The quenching effect of FA on the QDs PL emission was directly related with the quencher concentration according to the Stern-Volmer equation F0/F = 2.07 (±0.09) [FA] + 1.29 (±0.09).

Considering the interaction of Fe(II) with CA-CdTe QDs, two opposite concentration dependent effects on the PL intensity, were observed. In fact, for lower Fe(II) concentrations of up to 0.35 μmol L−1, the QDs exhibited a PL enhancement (Fig. S4 (a) – Supplementary material). The main reason for this improvement can be correlated to the occurrence of surface traps states that were passivated by low Fe(II) concentrations adsorbed at the QDs surface [18,25]. The PL enhancement of QDs caused by increasing Fe(II) concentrations was described by a Langmuir binding isotherm equation [Fe(II)]/FI = 3 × 10−10 (±1 × 10−10) + 4.2 × 10−8 (±1 × 10−9) [Fe(II)]. The equilibrium binding constant (B) was 6.21 × 1018 L μmol−1, which indicates a good surface affinity between the metal ion and the nanoparticle. The LOD calculated was 0.02 μmol L−1. In an opposite way, for iron concentrations between 0.5 up to 4.61 μmol L−1, the PL intensity of QDs was inhibited and accompanied by a hypsochromic effect of the maximum emission wavelength of about 11 nm (Fig. S4 (b) – Supplementary material). A linear relationship between the quenching effect and concentration of metal of up to 4.6 μmol L−1 was observed which was described by the following equation: F0/F = 1.38 (±0.09) [Fe(II)] + 1.07 (±0.08).

In order to understand if the blue shift was attributed to QDs size reduction or due to band gap variation, the QDs absorption spectra for increasing FA and Fe(II) concentrations, were assessed. The obtained results demonstrated no significant changes in the wavelength of maximum absorbance. Therefore, it was concluded that the QD size did not significantly change and the mechanism sustaining the hypochromic effect should be attributed to band gap variation.

Although CA-QDs exhibit two opposite effects upon interaction with iron, the PL behaviour exhibited in the presence of the metal and vitamin B9 was similar (PL quenching and a blue shift of maximum emission). Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant obtained for FA (Ksv = 2.07 (±0.09) L μmol −1) was close to that obtained for Fe(II) (Ksv = 1.38 (±0.09) L μmol −1). Therefore, these QDs are not suitable for discriminating the two compounds in the same sample as they show similar behaviours.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of FA and Fe(II) with CYS-CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of FA and Fe(II) on the PL properties of CYS-QDs was also evaluated in solution at concentrations ranging from 0.05 to 15 μmol L−1 and 0.03–263 μmol L−1, respectively.

In the case of FA (Fig. 1 a), no significant variation on the PL intensity was observed. When adding increasing concentrations of Fe(II), the PL signal was quenched, which was accompanied by a narrowing of the emission band noticeable in the decrease of FWHM value. The results of the interaction of CYS QDs with the cation showed that a linear relationship for concentrations between 0.3 and 197.5 μmol L−1 described by the equation F0/F = 0.0140 (±0.0003) [Fe(II)] + 0.96 (±0.04) (Fig. 1b).
Considering the different reactivity of the two compounds, it was anticipated that CYS-CdTe QDs could be successfully exploited as a PL probe for the simultaneous determination of these analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of FA and Fe(II) with MSA-CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: As it was observed in the previous QDs, the interaction of FA with MSA QDs revealed no significant variation on the PL properties (Fig. 2 a). On the other hand, Fe(II) caused a decrease of the PL intensity of the MSA QDs which was directly related with concentration for values ranging from 0.66 to 263 μmol L−1 (Fig. 2b). The linear relationship was described by Stern-Volmer equation, F0/F = 0.0031 (±0.0002) [Fe(II)] + 1.05 (±0.02).
The MSA QDs behaviour regarding the two analytes showed that this nanoparticle was suitable to be used as a PL probe for Fe(II) and vitamin B9 real-time determination in the same analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of FA and Fe(II) with MPA-CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this case, the interaction of MPA-CdTe with both FA and Fe(II) caused the quenching of the emission intensity at increasing concentrations of the two analytes (Fig. S5 – Supplementary material). For iron, two linear Stern-Volmer relationships F0/F = 0.00123 (±0.0005) [Fe(II)] + 1.06 (±0.02) and F0/F = 0.00161 (±0.0004) [Fe(II)] + 0.34 (±0.06) were observed for concentration ranges of 0.33–29.62 μmol L−1 and 53–263 μmol L−1, respectively. This can be ascribed to the different PL quenching process (dynamic and static) that could occur.

Concerning the interaction of MPA-QDs with FA, the linear relationship between PL quenching and concentration was verified for values ranging between 1.5 and 15 μmol L−1 and described by F0/F = 0.0065 (±0.0004) [FA] + 1.04 (±0.03).
Although MPA-QDs exhibited two different Stern-Volmer quenching constants upon interaction with Fe(II) and FA, the PL emission behaviours exhibited when adding the two analytes were very similar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of FA and Fe(II) with GSH-CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The results obtained upon interaction of GSH-CdTe QDs with FA showed a PL enhancement for low concentrations, between 0.1 and 10 μmol L−1 (Fig. 3 a). This effect can be ascribed to the passivation of the QDs surface by FA, which, by reducing the number of dangling bonds, promoted a removal of surface trap sites improving radiative recombination. The Langmuir binding isotherm equation that described the enhancement effect (Fig. 3b) was [FA]/FI = 6.94 × 10−7 (±8 × 10−9) + 3.2 × 10−7 (±5 × 10−8) [FA], in which the equilibrium binding constant was 1.41 × 1012 L μmol−1 and the LOD was 0.44 μmol L−1.

The interaction between GSH QDs and Fe(II) demonstrated a quenching effect on the PL probe emission (Fig. 3c), in which a linear Stern-Volmer relationship (F0/F = 0.0043 (±0.0004) [Fe(II)] + 1.12 (±0.04)) was verified for ion concentrations between 0.33 and 263.3 μmol L−1 (Fig. 3d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL quenching mechanism of QDs in presence of FA and Fe(II)
ce:section.ce:para: The PL quenching of CA-CdTe, MPA-CdTe and MPA-AIS QDs caused by FA can be ascribed to their antioxidant properties that makes FA a good reducing agent and therefore can easily trigger electron-hole transfer reactions with QDs. Some works in scientific literature reported that photoinduced electron transfer between QDs and FA was favored by electrostatic interactions between the involved species which prevent the radiative electron-hole recombination [11,18,65,66].

Regarding the PL quenching of the QDs in the presence of Fe(II), the probable mechanism was related to the binding of the cation at the QDs surface which promotes the electron transfer process between them causing disruption of radiative electron-hole recombination and leading to the decrease of PL efficiency [67,68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of a combined probe for the simultaneous determination of FA and Fe(II)
Optimization of the combined binary and ternary QDs probes
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the distinct reactivity that was detected between the assayed QDs and both FA and Fe(II), a characteristic emission spectrum profile for each analyte could be obtained upon the combined utilization of two or more QDs, of different nature, capped with distinct thiol-based compounds and emitting at different wavelengths, in the same measurement. With this purpose, and since CYS-CdTe and MSA-CdTe QDs showed no significant variation on their PL properties upon interaction with FA and demonstrated an inhibition of emission intensity when interacting with Fe(II), they were selected to develop a combined PL probe with AIS ternary QDs. These Cd-free QDs were also selected because they have different optical properties relatively to binary QDs, and thus distinct reactivity towards the analytes under evaluation.

Therefore, two different combined nanoprobes were implemented consisting on a mixture of each of the CdTe QDs with AgInS2 QDs. In the case of the CYS-CdTe/MPA- AgInS2 mingled probe, the resulting PL spectra showed two well-defined emission peaks as represented in Fig. 4 a. When comparing the combined spectra profile with the individual spectra of each QDs, a slight bathochromic effect of the maximum emission wavelength of CYS-CdTe QDs (from 569 to 585 nm) was observed while a hypsochromic effect was verified in the PL emission of AIS QDs (from 703 to 680 nm).

On the other hand, the emission peaks of the MSA-CdTe/MPA-AIS combined probe were not well defined since the maximum emission wavelengths of each involved nanoparticle were too proximate (Fig. 4b). The mixture of these two QDs probes resulted in an overlap of the emission spectra which led to an increase of PL intensity of both emission bands. Additionally, no significant shift effect of the maximum emission wavelengths was observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Individual interaction of FA and Fe(II) with combined QDs probes
ce:section.ce:para: The PL properties of each of the combined probes were evaluated upon individual interaction with FA and Fe(II) (Fig. 5 ).

It is worth mentioning that the stability of the binary and ternary individual QDs were effectively higher relatively to their mixture in a dual-emission probe. In fact, after the preparation of each individual binary and ternary QDs intermediate solutions, the PL properties remains unchanged for at least 12 h. However, when CdTe and AgInS2 QDs were mixed, the PL properties of the obtained dual-emission probe solution showed significant modifications after approximately 30 min and 1 h for CYS-CdTe/MPA-AIS and MSA-CdTe/MPA-AIS combined probes, respectively. In spite of this, the stability was not a problem from an analytical point of view because the spectra obtained from the interaction between each dual-emission probes and the analytes were recorded immediately after the mixture of both binary and ternary QDs which was within the stability period of each combined probe tested.

Moreover, in order to assess the reproducibility of the PL spectrum of the CdTe/AgInS2 dual emission probe in the absence of the analytes (blank assay), different spectra of the blank were repeatedly recorded throughout the assays involving the interaction of the combined probe with the analytes. From the analysis of the obtained spectra, one can observe the high reproducibility of the blank assays which guarantees the reliability of the results.

In the case of the interaction of FA with CYS-CdTe/MPA-AIS probe, the results showed that only the emission band corresponding to MPA-AIS was quenched and no significant variation was verified for CYS-CdTe (Fig. 5a). On the other hand, the interaction with Fe(II) showed a PL inhibition of both emission bands (Fig. 5b).

In the interaction of FA with MSA-CdTe/MPA-AIS probe, the obtained results revealed that the PL intensity of both emission bands was inhibited (Fig. 5c). However, it should be emphasised that the quenching of the MSA-CdTe emission mostly resulted from of the decrease of MPA-AIS QDs photoluminescence. In effect, as it was abovementioned, the PL emission of the binary QDs increased when these were mixed with ternary ones due to the overlap of both spectra. Therefore, as it was noticed in preliminary assays, only the ternary QDs showed significant PL variation upon FA interaction and for that reason the observed lessening of the binary QDs emission was ascribed to the PL quenching of MPA-AIS. Regarding the interaction of this combined probe with Fe(II), the PL intensity of both emission bands was quenched being more accentuated in the case of MPA-AIS QDs (Fig. 5d).

Accordingly, all results of the interaction of FA and Fe(II) with both combined probes were in accordance with those obtained in the interaction of the analytes with each individual probe. Taking into account the obtained spectrum profile the CYS-CdTe/MPA-AIS combined probe was selected for the simultaneous determination of FA and Fe(II) since it allowed an enhanced analyte discrimination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous interaction of FA and Fe(II) with combined probe
ce:section.ce:para: Aiming at determining FA and Fe(II) in a binary mixture and in real samples, an experimental mixture design of 30 samples encompassing the interaction of the combined nanoprobe with binary mixtures of FA and Fe(II) at concentration levels of 7–17 and 5–45 μmol L−1, respectively, was performed. In addition, 3 more samples were included, specifically, a blank (nanoprobe spectrum in the absence of analytes), a sample containing only Fe(II) at the maximum concentration level and a third one consisting of a FA solution at the maximum concentration (Table S1 – Supplementary material). As abovementioned, the obtained spectra were firstly analysed through PCA using Hotelling’s T2 and Q residuals statistics which revealed the absence of outliers. Therefore, all data was considered for further processing through PLS and ANN modelling, aiming to apply the more efficient data analysis model for the simultaneous quantification of both analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of PLS and ANN models
ce:section.ce:para: During PLS optimization, several pre-processing techniques were applied as described in 2.6 section. The best pre-processing technique for the determination of both analytes consisted on the application of log10 transformation. The obtained results for the best models are shown in Table 2 .

The results indicated that the PLS model for Fe(II) determination was acceptable for quality control (RER >10) while for FA was only acceptable for screening purposes (RER < 9) although the R2 P being higher than 0.9.
The plots of the predicted values versus the experimental values for FA and Fe(II) are depicted in Fig. 6 .

The PLS regression analysis obtained for FA (Fig. 6a) suggested that the relationship between fluorescence data and FA concentration was non-linear. Indeed, looking carefully to Fig. 6a, the depicted points seem to follow a sigmoid function. Additionally, the best pre-processing technique used for both analytes was obtained through the application of log10 transformation of the fluorescence data which also reinforced the idea of non-linearity. In this sense, ANN was tested for the development of quantification models for FA and Fe(II), as it is suitable for handling non-linear data, and also aiming at improving the accuracy of the developed models. As mentioned before, the optimization of ANN involved testing different input and hidden layer neurons number (by trial and error for both analytes) and several pre-processing techniques. Four input layer neurons, two hidden layer neurons and one single output neuron with the data just mean centered yielded the best results without over-fitting both analytes models. The obtained results for the best ANN models are shown in Table 3 .
The plots of the training (a and c), monitoring (a and c) and predicted values obtained by ANN modelling versus the experimental values (b and d) are depicted in Fig. 7 .

The results obtained through ANN were statistically better, in terms of RMSE, for the FA determination than those obtained through PLS (p < 0.05). Regarding the determination of Fe(II), ANN results were slightly better, in terms of RMSE, than those obtained through PLS but not statistically better (p > 0.05). In this context, ANN modelling revealed to be more suitable than PLS, which dictated its selection for their simultaneous quantification in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of Fe(II) and FA in commercial samples
ce:section.ce:para: Two commercial samples were analysed, in the conditions described in section 2.1, in order to verify the accuracy of the ANN modelling. The ANN models parameters for the quantification of Fe(II) and FA were the same as used before in section 3.4.1. The results obtained are shown in Table 4 and Fig. 8 .

The obtained results revealed (Table 4 and Table S2 – Supplementary material) that the determination of both analytes in commercial samples was successfully performed considering the obtained SEP% values below 10%. Moreover, the obtained R2 P and LOQ values for both analytes were above 0.9 and below the lowest dilution tested, respectively. This demonstrates that the use of combined nanoprobe coupled to chemometrics models can be an interesting alternative for the determination of at least two distinct active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) in the same commercial sample, showing that PLS and ANN can circumvent the lack of selectivity of the dual-emission probe. Both models are able to achieve the first-order advantage, which allows to perform determinations of analytes of interest in the presence of interfering substances, since those interferents are included in the calibration samples [69,70].
The optimization study and the simultaneous quantification of FA and Fe(II) in pharmaceutical formulations are summarized in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: The PL modulation endured by the assayed CdTe QDs (with distinct surface chemistry) upon interaction with folic acid and Fe(II) was of dissimilar magnitude demonstrating the importance of selecting appropriate thiol-based capping ligands for achieving fine-tuning nanoparticle reactivity thus allowing refinement of both sensitivity and selectivity. This behaviour, coupled with the use of ternary AIS QDs, also with specific reactivity, allowed the implementation of a combined nanoprobe seeking to obtain specific analyte-response profiles and guaranteeing a far better discrimination of the analytes under evaluation.

This work demonstrated that the synergy resulting from combining different QDs in a dual emission probe and by processing the obtained data with chemometric tools allowed to effectively circumvent the selectivity issues arising from the use of highly reactive quantum dots for the determination of multiple analytes in the same sample analysis.

The application of chemometric tools revealed a non-linear relationship between the concentration of FA and the fluorescence data obtained from the interaction of this molecule with the combined probe. In this sense, ANN modelling showed better results than those obtained by PLS due to its ability to tackle non-linear data (Scheme 1A). The ANN results demonstrated the accuracy of the developed methodology for the simultaneous determination of FA and Fe(II) in calibration samples and in real samples (Scheme 1B). It should be highlighted, that SEP% values below 10% were obtained for both analytes as well as for the analysis of the two commercial pharmaceutical formulations under evaluation, which attested the accuracy of the developed ANN models. To the best of our knowledge, this was the first time that the combination of different QDs with chemometric tools, namely ANN, was successfully applied in pharmaceutical analysis.
